• In 2013, the Centers for Medicare and Medicaid Services (CMS) 
developed a national quality strategy (NQS) aligned with three 
broad aims: better care, affordable care, and healthy people

• The evidence-based triad helps clinicians decide on applicability 
of evidence to individual patients by seeking common ground in 
the triad of (1) clinical impression based on individual clinical ex-
pertise, (2) best available external evidence, and (3) the patient’s 
unique values and expectations.
• Outcome measures used in the field of rehabilitation are met -
rics that have been developed and validated to track and report 
outcomes of clinical care (clinical outcome metrics), clinical care 
process (process outcome metrics), and functional change 
(functional outcome metrics).
• Performance measures used in rehabilitation, reported often as 
a ratio or a percentage, are used to monitor how well the 
health care system achieves what it purports to achieve, with a 
focus on quality, cost, efficiency, effectiveness, disparities, 
safety, and accreditation reporting. Examples include length of

SUMMARY PEARLS
8
Quality and Outcome Measures for 
Medical Rehabilitation
WEIBIN YANG, PRATEEK GROVER, AND GARGI D. RAVAL
Optimal health care quality can be defined as the provision of safe 
and effective care that improves outcomes, optimizes health, re -
sults in high patient satisfaction, and improves quality of life. As 
such, the delivery of high-quality care should be a top priority for 
all rehabilitation professionals. In the United States and world -
wide, the evaluation, measurement, and reporting of quality and 
outcomes have become the business not only of every health care 
institution and hospital but also of every individual health care 
provider. This chapter discusses health care quality, quality im -
provement, evidence-based medicine (EBM), clinical practice 
guidelines (CPGs), outcome measures, performance measures, 
practice improvement, patient safety, accreditation, and physician 
continuing board certification.
Access, affordability, and high-quality health care were the 
main objectives of the Patient Protection and Affordable Care Act 
(ACA) of 2010. T o accomplish these goals, the Department of 
Health and Human Services outlined a national quality strategy 
(NQS). In November 2013, the Centers for Medicare and Med -
icaid Services (CMS) developed a quality strategy, CMS Quality 
Strategy 2013–Beyond, which was aligned with the three broad 
aims of NQS: better care, healthy people/healthy communities, 
and affordable care. In 2022, CMS launched the new CMS NQS, 
a plan aimed at improving the quality and safety of health care for 
everyone, with a special focus on people from underserved and 
underresourced communities. CMS’s quality strategy goals are 
depicted in Fig. 8.1. The new CMS NQS has four priority areas, 
each with two goals. In both versions of CMS NQS, improving 
safety and quality of care are always the main goals. T o achieve 
these goals, CMS and other health care insurance providers rely 
on the simple, meaningful, efficient, and accurate measurement of 
health care quality and safety delivered by providers.
In addition to the CMS, several other governmental and many 
nongovernmental agencies monitor and oversee health care quality 
and safety. For example, the National Committee for Quality 
 Assurance (NCQA) is a nonprofit organization focusing on health 
care quality. Health insurance plans all over the United States seek 
and obtain the NCQA seal of approval, which reflects their high-
quality health care and service. According to the NCQA, its accred-
ited plans cover more than 197 million Americans. 42 The quality 
standards and performance measures used by the NCQA and other 
similar agencies are tools that individual clinicians and organizations 
use to self-report the quality and safety of health care they have de-
livered. These measures are then used (1) to compare against local, 
regional, or national standards; (2) by consumers (patients), media, 
and other members of the public to compare clinicians, plans, and 
institutions against each other; and (3) to compare similar scores 
over time to identify opportunities for improvement, such as design-
ing practice and performance improvement projects.

125Chapter 8  Quality and Outcome Measures for Medical Rehabilitation
An important concept in health care quality is that of value. 
Despite a common objective of improving performance in health 
care delivery, there are several stakeholders in health care that 
sometimes have conflicting goals. This can lead to divergent ap -
proaches to improving health care quality caused by a lack of 
clarity and cohesion among stakeholders, resulting in a slow rate 
of progress. Porter suggests that the achievement of high value 
should be the overarching goal of health care delivery regardless of 
one’s perspective or role.
48 Value, in this instance, was defined as 
health outcomes achieved per dollar spent. Health care outcomes 
were divided into three tiers: tier 1: health status achieved or re-
tained; tier 2: process of recovery; and tier 3: sustainability of 
health. Porter recommends that the measurement of “valuable” 
medical outcomes in any medical condition or disease must in-
clude at least one measure in each tier.
27,48
Quality of care is a measure of performance on the six health 
care aims specified by the Institute of Medicine (IOM): (1) safety, 
(2) timeliness, (3) effectiveness, (4) equity, (5) efficiency, and  
(6) patient centeredness. Safety refers to “freedom from accidental 
injury” and entails reducing errors. An error is the failure of a 
planned action to be completed as intended or the use of a wrong 
plan to achieve an aim. Based on performance metrics, providers, 
individual organizations, and health plans are incentivized to 
improve quality through accountability, thereby achieving the 
IOM’s six aims.
30 Efforts to accurately and efficiently measure 
quality continue to grow rapidly. These measures are introduced 
to and adopted by health plans and clinicians based on several 
factors.
Quality measures need to be meaningful and relevant to pa -
tients and society as well as valid and based on sound evidence. The 
measurement of quality must be transparent, fair, and  r eliable. 
Reliability prevents accidents by utilizing design and management 
principles to reduce conditions that can lead to  err ors. T ranspar-
ency supports safety by making the reliability of  organizations

visible by offering comparisons through public reporting of perfor-
mance measures. Quality measurement must not place too much 
additional burden on clinicians, their institutions, or health plans, 
and providing high-quality and safe care must be appropriately 
rewarded and incentivized. Fig. 8.2 illustrates a model of how evi-
dence-based clinical research translates into quality measures that 
eventually lead to health improvement.  I deally, all quality measures 
are based on the highest possible quality of scientific evidence, but 
some measures are adopted because they are in the best interest of 
consumers and the society at large even if they are based on best 
available evidence and not the best possible evidence. The ultimate 
objective is to deliver high-quality health care and rehabilitation 
and to improve the health of the population we serve. What one 
can do to turn the gears of the quality machine depends on the role 
played in the quality arena. One way is to conceptualize the inter-
play between how efforts at providing high-quality health care can 
vary depending on one’s perspective and role in the health care 
system (Fig. 8.3). One example of the effort to improve health care 
quality is the medical specialty board certification process and 
continuing certification process.
The Value Equation
Quality measurement dates to Avedis Donabedian’s paper22 that 
categorizes quality into structure, process, and outcomes. 
 Measurement to improve quality began in 1998, when The Joint 
Commission launched its ORYX initiative, the first national 
program for the measurement of hospital quality. Subsequently, in 
2011, a study was published in the Journal of Hospital Medicine  
based on the study of 4000 hospitals from 2004 to 2008 for per -
formance trends and The Joint Commission association. The 
study showed that The Joint Commission–accredited hospitals 
outperformed nonaccredited hospitals in nationally standardized 
quality measures such as heart failure and pneumonia. But as

(either directly or by reducing the risk of adverse outcomes).
 2. Proximity. The process being measured must be connected to 
the outcome it affects.
 3. Accuracy. The measure should be able to assess whether an ev-
idence-based process is effectively delivered for improvement 
to occur.
 4. Adverse effects. The measure should minimize or eliminate un-
intended adverse effects.
Measures that meet all four criteria are now used for accredita-
tion, public reporting, and pay-for-performance reviews. Perfor -
mance measurement with these accountability measures has 
transformed passive payers and consumers into purchasers of ef -
ficient high-quality health care, that is value-based performance, 
where value 5 quality or outcomes/cost.
Seemingly simple, this equation has a potential to cause confu-
sion if it is not understood that the denominator “cost” refers to 
the total costs of the full episode of care, not the cost of individual 
services.
13,48 Measuring value in the context of improvement in the 
value of health care by way of performance metrics allows decisions 
on which services need more spending so that the need for others 
is reduced. Physiatrists, regardless of whether practicing inpatient 
rehabilitation or outpatient musculoskeletal and spine care, need 
to be aware of the shift in health care’s focus from volume to value-
based care.
13 T oday, the higher cost of care is also associated with a 
poorer quality of care; however, it is imperative to be aware that 
improvement in value cannot be done by cost containment alone. 
A cultural change involving innovation and accountability is criti-
cal to improving value in health care delivery. Such an improve -
ment is possible with performance measurement because of the 
transparency with which performance metrics are reported.
Outcome Measures
Broadly speaking, an outcome is the impact of an activity that is 
driven by input and results in an output (logic model).

The value of care is now an important consideration because

Therefore the focus of measurement has shifted to accountabil -

127Chapter 8  Quality and Outcome Measures for Medical Rehabilitation
care, an example is a clinical outcome, defined as “a health state 
of a patient resulting from health care” 1 by the National Quality 
Measures Clearinghouse of the Agency of Healthcare Research 
and Quality (AHRQ). Measuring such outcomes is vital for track-
ing and refinement purposes and is accomplished by using metrics 
called outcome measures. For clinical care, clinical outcome mea-
sures assess clinical outcomes through qualitative or quantitative 
means. Outcome measures are selected based on relevance of 
outcomes to patients, providers, organizations, and health care 
systems. Examples include positive outcomes such as improved 
function and negative outcomes such as hospital-acquired skin 
breakdown. In the health care context, outcome measures can be 
loosely categorized as clinical outcome measures and process 
 measures.
Clinical Outcome and Process Measures
Donabedian conceptualized health care quality evaluation and 
improvement in 1966 using three dimensions: structure, process, 
and outcomes.
22 Structure is the context in which health care is 
delivered (e.g., the buildings, equipment, resources, organiza -
tional characteristics). The structure of health care is often impli -
cated as an upstream cause of a problem. For example, the 
 previous US health care structure rewarded intervention, and it 
has been modified to reward value instead to minimize unneces -
sary interventions. The process of health care is the additive sum 
of all actions and activities involved in the delivery of health care 
services. This includes how patients access health care, how pa -
tients are assessed, and how diagnoses are made; the way in which 
treatments are rendered; and the activities involved in counseling, 
health education, and health promotion. For example, the process 
of making a diagnosis involves information gathering, conducting 
an examination, synthesizing data, eliciting additional informa-
tion in the form of testing or additional consultations, and mak -
ing an assessment. From Donabedian’s perspective, the outcome 
is the effect of health care on a patient or population. Because it 
is difficult to ascertain if an outcome is the direct result of health 
care, it is often challenging to accurately measure if and how 
much of an outcome is attributable to health care structure or 
process. Nonetheless, achieving a specific outcome is the reason 
specific health care structures are designed and processes take 
place, and therefore outcomes must be measured. Fig. 8.4 illus-
trates Donabedian’s concept with some rehabilitation-relevant 
examples included in each dimension.
With regard to the appropriate selection and utilization of an 
outcome measure, it is important to first determine whether the 
measure has been evaluated for validity (how well the test mea -
sures what it intends to measure) and reliability (the degree  
to which the measure produces consistent and stable results). 
 Consider the following example: A patient with spinal cord injury 
is diagnosed with a urinary tract infection (UTI) based on the 
presence of greater than 100,000 colony-forming units (CFUs) 
on urine culture. The patient is prescribed a course of antibiotics; 
on repeat urine culture 2 weeks later, greater than 100,000 CFUs 
are again found. The goal of the treatment with antibiotics was to 
eliminate the UTI. Based on the outcome measure of choice in 
this example—repeat urine culture (a reliable and valid way to 
measure UTI)—the outcome was not achieved.
The question then becomes why was the outcome not achieved? 
It could be because of a deficit health care structure, such as 
poorly functioning equipment that provided incorrect results, or 
in the process, such as presumptively prescribing an antibiotic and 
not missing the opportunity to modify it based on culture results. 
Alternatively, the patient may have taken the prescribed antibiotic 
for a day and then stopped or may not have been able to afford 
the copay. Or, as is often the case, a combination of health care 
factors and patient factors may have affected the outcome. Even 
in the situation where the patient prematurely stopped taking the 
prescribed medication, there may have been health care process 
factors contributing to the poor outcome, such as failure on the 
part of the provider to explain how to take the medication or 
failure on the part of the pharmacist to label the medication in the 
patient’s native language. Implementing standard health care pro-
cesses, such as a telephone or text check with the patient, could 
have helped to improve patient adherence. As evidenced by this 
example, even when the management plan is well defined and the 
evidence behind the treatment is standard of care, achieving a 
particular health care outcome can be challenging.
Because processes impact outcomes, providers and hospitals 
measure both processes and outcomes to comprehensively  monitor

SECTION 1  Evaluation
and control both clinical outcomes and fidelity with processes. For 
example, a rehabilitation unit might be seeking to reduce pulmo-
nary emboli (a negative outcome) and have parameters set for who 
should be screened using lower extremity Doppler ultrasound. The 
associated process measure of appropriate screening would be the 
percentage of those screened among those who met the screening 
criteria. The higher the percentage, the better. Ideally, all who 
should have been screened would be, resulting in a 100% score for 
this measure. Certainly, the rehabilitation unit would also measure 
the number of patients who had pulmonary embolism (i.e., the 
clinical outcome). In this case, the goal would be to have zero cases 
of pulmonary embolism among the at-risk population (the out -
come measure). However, the rehabilitation unit leadership might 
prefer to utilize process measures in addition for several reasons. 
First, pulmonary embolism is rare, so only a few patients would 
have pulmonary embolism, and this would not alone reflect the 
unit’s quality of care. Second, the severity and condition type of 
the patients might be such that the patients on this unit might be 
at higher risk for pulmonary embolism than those on other units, 
making a comparison on this outcome measure unfair. Third, the 
leadership might recognize that the process measure would be 
much more tangible to the staff and something they could con -
cretely set goals around.
Another measure relevant to the rehabilitation provider is the 
rate of transfers from an inpatient rehabilitation facility (IRF) 
unit back to acute care (i.e., the acute care transfer [ACT] rate).60 
The ACT rate is used as a measure of postacute care quality be -
cause these “bounce backs” are common and costly to the health 
care system,36 especially in the face of vertical integration since the 
passage of the ACA. 53 Just as 30-day readmission rates represent 
the quality of acute care services, high ACT rates can be represen-
tative of inferior postacute care quality because some ACTs could 
be potentially preventable.41 A high ACT rate from postacute care 
or 30-day readmission rate on the acute care side could indicate 
that the health status of the population served was not stable 
enough for transferring from acute to postacute care and did not 
achieve its intended goal of assuring health. However, the reasons 
why patients are readmitted are many, including some that are 
well beyond the control of the hospital process during the original 
admission. In addition, most readmissions may be appropriate. 
Therefore, when process measure goals are being set for an IRF , it 
is necessary to consider the underlying reasons for return to the 
acute hospital from that IRF . Measuring the clinical outcome is 
hence not enough to make informed changes to the readmissions 
process. Measures capturing the steps of the care process can en -
sure a systematic approach to improving outcome and explain 
why process measures are chosen.
Selecting an appropriate process measure requires strong scien-
tific evidence linking the process to the outcome. As an example, 
handwashing reduces nosocomial infections; therefore the degree 
of adherence to hand hygiene policy is an appropriate process 
measure linked to nosocomial infections. Additionally, there are 
no obvious contraindications to hand hygiene, so the process 
measure goal would be 100%. This contrasts with the previous 
example of ACT rate or 30-day readmission rate, for which a 
target goal of 0% would be unrealistic and inappropriate. Another 
important consideration in selecting a process measure is the ease 
of data collection and analysis. In the era of electronic medical 
records (EMRs), queries of care processes and steps are much 
easier to conduct. Examples of EMR process evaluation strategies 
include keyword searches, categorization by diagnostic codes, or -
der tracking, and provider compliance monitoring. Overall, the 
use of process measures in combination with outcome measures 
helps drive quality improvement in a more systematic fashion 
than focusing on outcome measures alone. Finally, it is important 
to adjust for risk factors and/or stratify the population (risk 
stratification) when using outcome measures by using statistical 
adjustment of risk techniques. In summary, it is essential to mea-
sure both outcomes of health care delivery (outcome measures) 
and the underlying process (process measures) to ensure that the 
system is optimized to promote the health of the population 
served.
Functional Outcome Measures
The International Classification of Functioning, Disability and 
Health (ICF), released in 2001 as an update by the World Health 
Organization to their International Classification of Impairments, 
Disabilities and Handicaps, 33 defines function as an interplay 
between three levels: at the level of the body part or system (im -
pairment), at the level of the person (activity), and at the level of 
social role (participation), with influence from an individual’s 
health conditions interacting with the particular environment  
and personal factors (contextual factors). These environmental 
and personal factors mediate function at each level in positive and 
negative ways.35
Functional outcomes 59 are useful to measure the baseline for 
a particular level as well as the postintervention change in re -
sponse to interventions implemented. The goal of an interven -
tion may be to alleviate pain or improve range of motion (body 
function and structure), to improve walking (an activity), or to 
improve the patient’s ability to work to avoid loss of employment 
(participation), or a combination of all three. As an example, a 
knee joint injection may improve the patient’s pain, which in 
turn could improve ambulation and therefore the ability to par -
ticipate in work. In contrast, using a walker may directly affect 
ambulation and allow for improved work attendance but does 
not affect knee function. Recommending worksite adaptations 
may improve participation but not the patient’s body function or 
ability to ambulate. Because there is not always a clear linear 
causal relationship between the levels of functioning, there is no 
guarantee that a prescribed intervention that successfully im -
pacted one level of functioning will positively affect another. 
Similarly, if contextual factors—namely, environmental and per -
sonal factors—are not taken into consideration, functional out -
come measures may not accurately reflect the results of health 
care delivery.
Using the ICF model’s functional level as a framework, most 
medical specialties measure impairments at the body function 
and structures level, whereas physical medicine and rehabilita -
tion (PM&R) and other rehabilitation disciplines additionally 
measure activities with assessment instruments, such as the 
functional independence measure (FIM) and Continuity 
 Assessment Record and Evaluation (CARE) tool/Section GG, 58 
as well as participation with quality of life tools. Functional 
outcome measures can be further subcategorized in several 
ways: (1) whether they are reported by the patient, such as the 
health-related quality of life, or by the provider, such as the 
FIM; (2) whether they are collected by subjective assessment, 
such as FIM, or by objective measurement, such as Timed Up 
and Go test; and (3) whether they can be diagnosis agnostic, 
such as FIM, or diagnosis specific, such as Amputation  Mobility 
 Predictor (AMPPro) for functional Medicare K-level assess -
ment for persons with limb loss.

129Chapter 8 Quality and Outcome Measures for Medical Rehabilitation
Functional Independence Measure and CARE Tool/ 
Section GG
In the past, most inpatient rehabilitation programs submitted 
their patients’ FIM scores to the Uniform Data System (UDS). 
The FIM instrument for adults and the WeeFIM instrument for 
children measured function in self-care, sphincter control, trans -
fers, locomotion, communication, and social cognition using 18 
different items. These items were scored on a 1-to-7 ordinal scale 
based on help needed from another person to complete the tasks, 
with a score of 1 indicating total assistance needed and a score of 
7 indicating complete independence. UDS provided report cards 
that detailed FIM change (discharge FIM/admission FIM) and 
FIM efficiency (FIM change/length of stay) for patients by im -
pairment grouping. UDS also tracked the percentage of patients 
successfully discharged home. These data were also stratified by 
impairment type, age, sex, and insurance status. It is important to 
note that the change in function a population of patients experi -
ences during rehabilitation could be caused by a host of factors 
outside the control of the rehabilitation physician. Therefore FIM 
change and FIM efficiency could be considered better outcome 
measures for the rehabilitation facility than the rehabilitation 
physician. That being said, there are statistical methods of con -
trolling other factors that would allow for one physician’s patients’ 
FIM change and efficiency scores to be compared to other physi -
cians’ scores. This would allow for physician-level benchmarking, 
but it requires a large sample size.
Although much of the rehabilitation literature has utilized the 
FIM to evaluate functional change until recently, the FIM was 
replaced by the CARE item/Section GG in October 2019. The 
CARE item set was developed as a standardized patient assess -
ment tool58 for use in postacute settings. 57 The CARE tool stan-
dardizes the medical, functional, cognitive, and social support 
status assessments for individual patients and includes both core 
items (assessed on all patients) and supplemental items (based on 
condition) that assess severity or degree of need. In contrast to the 
FIM, the CARE tool 58 measures level of assistance required on a 
scale of 1 to 6. Similar to the FIM, the CARE tool is used to track 
individual outcomes, institutional outcomes, and population 
outcomes with stratification by patient-level factors such as condi-
tion type or severity.
Patient-Reported Outcome Metrics
The Patient-Reported Outcomes Measurement Information  System 
(PROMIS) was created by the National Institutes of Health (NIH) 
to provide several validated, reliable, and precise patient-reported 
outcome measures.28 These can be used to understand the self-  
reported health status of patients’ physical, mental, and social well-
being, especially for chronic disease states and across a wide range 
of demographics. PROMIS has a core set of questions designed to 
assess common patient-relevant outcomes across a wide range of 
conditions and specific item banks that focus on areas such as pain, 
fatigue, and physical functioning. Depending on the time available 
and level of detail desired, PROMIS instruments of varying length 
(e.g., 10, 29, and 57 items) are available.
A particularly attractive feature of PROMIS is the use of item 
response theory and computerized adaptive testing (CAT). Unlike 
a traditional questionnaire that is the same for all patients, a CAT 
instrument uses statistical modeling to determine the most ap -
propriate follow-up question to ask a patient based on response to 
an initial question, hence tailoring questions specific to each pa -
tient.34 If a patient’s response to an initial question indicates the 
ability to complete a task with little help, the CAT selects the next 
question with a more challenging task than the first. If that re -
sponse indicates higher difficulty in completing the more chal -
lenging task, the CAT selects the following question with an easier 
task than in the second question. This process continues until the 
computer identifies that the preprogrammed confidence interval 
has been met or the patient has answered the maximal number of 
items used to estimate the score.34
Pediatric Clinical Outcome, Process,  
and Functional Outcome Metrics
Similar to adults, pediatric measures can be broadly considered 
under clinical outcome, process, and functional metrics. The 
AHRQ has quality indicators2 for children receiving care in hos -
pitals, some of which—such as the number of stage III or IV 
pressure injuries as a secondary diagnosis per 1000 discharges or 
the number of central venous catheter-related bloodstream infec -
tions per 1000—are particularly relevant to rehabilitation care. 
Similarly, for the rehabilitation population, the outcome of venti-
lator-associated pneumonia is of particular interest, as hospital 
complications can lead to worsening debility and a delay in reha -
bilitative care.
Functional outcomes metrics used for adults may not be a 
good fit for assessing outcomes for children. The measurement of 
pediatric functional outcomes should be developmentally appro -
priate, as self-care, cognitive, and mobility expectations change 
over the course of childhood. Because children live within the 
context of their families, it is also important to consider the mea -
surement of family function, health, and well-being. Additionally, 
patient-reported outcome measures should allow for child report-
ing as well as parental reporting to assess simultaneous child and 
parent perspectives.
Choosing Outcome Measures
Choosing an outcome measure is not an exact science because of 
the plethora of measures available, with varying levels of valida -
tion across factors such as diagnosis, age, sex, and care setting.  
A good starting place for rehabilitation professionals to systemati-
cally select outcome measures is the Rehabilitation Measures 
 Database.54 This is a publicly available comprehensive collection 
of evidence-based outcome measures that can be used by clini -
cians to measure clinical progress for individual patients and by 
researchers for groups of patients for study design and assessment. 
Examples of outcome measures in various topic areas within 
PM&R are shown in Table 8.1.
Major considerations when deciding upon an outcome mea -
sure include the nature and utility of the measure for the clini -
cian and the patient, whether the measure has been validated for 
the specified group of patients, how much of the outcome is 
 directly attributable to the intervention ( Box 8.1), and the feasi -
bility of using a metric for the particular application. The latter 
relates to factors such as ease of learning, ease of administration, 
and cost. Filters on the website to assist in measure selection 
 include measure type (e.g., observer, patient reported, perfor -
mance, physiologic, proxy), area of assessment (e.g., mobility, 
activities of daily living, executive functioning), population (e.g., 
stroke, spinal injury, osteoarthritis), body part (e.g., chest, lower 
extremity), and cost.
For example, the use of the 6-minute walk test (6MWT) may be 
a better indicator of the success of a pulmonary rehabilitation pro-
gram than the mobility section of the Craig Handicap  Assessment

Performance Measures and Quality 
Improvement
Performance measurement is a methodology to monitor how well 
the health care system achieves what it purports to achieve, with 
a focus on quality, cost, efficiency, effectiveness, disparities, safety, 
and accreditation reporting.29 A performance measure is generally 
a ratio or a percentage that compares providers and institutions

 Examples include length of stay, readmission rate, mortality rate, 
and patient satisfaction ratings.
Performance metrics have helped in transforming health care 
quality by embedding improvement methodologies within health 
care and consequently created an accountable data-driven health 
care organizational culture. Most of these metrics are reflective of 
collaborative multidisciplinary work involving physicians, nurses, 
therapists, pharmacists, and other clinicians and nonclinicians. 
Other stakeholders who drive this culture of quality and patient 
safety include local and regional purchasers, payers, consumer 
representatives, and communities.
Performance Measure Development
Development of performance metrics is an exceedingly long and 
resource-intensive process, requiring financial, human, techno -
logic, educational, and policy resources. Furthermore, adoption of 
these measures and their impact on care quality improvement are 
variable. The National Healthcare Quality Report (NHQR) re -
leased in May 2014 reported that 70% of recommended quality 
care was actually received, with wide variations among US states. 
Although the measures used in the report included a wide range 
of structure, process, and outcome measures, several important 
dimensions of quality were not currently measured. Some exam -
ples pertaining to rehabilitation medicine include (1) pain reduc-
tion and functional improvement in patients undergoing back 
surgery, total joint replacements, and other orthopedic proce -
dures, and (2) rate of decline in the function of patients with 
multiple sclerosis. T racking the rate of change over 4 years of data, 
the NHQR also reported that whereas the quality of hospital care 
was improving, ambulatory care was slower to improve. There are 
few measures to trend limitations in activity, and fewer showed 
improvement in activity caused by the larger standard errors of 
estimates of people with activity limitations. Not only was the gap 
between the best possible care and routinely delivered care sub -
stantial, but access to health care also worsened from 2002 to 
2010, leveling off in 2011. These statistics from NHQR track 
health care quality and report everyday experiences of patients 
and their providers across the nation. The number of people with

Considerations for Performance Measure 
Development
The development of performance measures is difficult for several 
reasons, which are discussed next.
The Desirable Attributes of a Measure Are Complex
By consensus from the American Medical Association (AMA), 
The Joint Commission, and NCQA, a performance measure must 
(1) address a topic area that is of high priority to maximize the 
health of persons or populations, is financially important, and 
demonstrates a variation in care and/or potential improvement; 
(2) have usefulness in improving patient outcomes based on es -
tablished clinical recommendations, be potentially actionable, 
and be meaningful to and interpretable by the user; and (3) have 
a measure design with well-defined specifications, documented 
reliability, and validity and that allows risk while being feasible, 
confidential, and publicly available.
Measure Testing Is Elaborate
Measure testing must be conducted in a variety of practice set -
tings. For example, ambulatory measures must be evaluated in 
solo to large practices. Measure testing requires the involvement 
of all stakeholders, patients, and providers, including insurers, 
technology providers, individual or groups of clinicians, and 
health care organizations.
Measures Need to Meet Multiple Criteria
The AMA’s Physician’s Consortium for Process Improvement 
(PCPI)47 recommends that performance measures be tested using 
several criteria, including (1) needs assessment, (2) feasibility and 
implementation, (3) reliability, (4) validity, (5) unintended conse-
quences, and (6) applications.
Measure Development Life Cycle Is Expensive
The life cycle of a measure, as endorsed by the National Quality 
Forum,43 an entity that sets priorities for performance measure -
ment nationally, entails (1) studying evidence-based data on 
 disease prevalence, (2) using an assessment tool to study disease 
severity, (3) using local initiative to measure improvement with 
process, (4) leading change toward improvement locally, (5) pro -
viding public reporting on local performance, (6) endorsing the 
measure as national consensus standard, (7) retooling the measure 
for use in electronic health records, and (8) ensuring inclusion in 
CMS’s Meaningful Use program so that the measure adoption is 
widespread and leads to improvement in patient care.
Risk Adjustment Is Needed to Adapt to Context
Hospitals and provider organizations serve patients with varying 
risk factors.38 The risk profile can be considered as a combination 
of patient characteristics, such as age, sex, race, admitting diagnoses,

these risk factors to calculate a numeric value that can be compared

be a complicated and long-drawn process.
Performance Reporting Databases Require Both 
Transparency and Privacy
Public reporting of performance measures involves systems and 
methods to simultaneously maintain transparency and protect 
patient privacy.31 These measures are reported from electronic 
health records to public reporting organizations, such as CMS, 
that maintain large databases of these measures. This process is 
complex, with multiple stakeholders and cross-cutting measures, 
making measuring performance a necessary but challenging task.
Strategically Improving Health Care Quality With 
Performance Measurement and Performance 
Improvement
Despite challenges, performance measurement is imperative be -
cause it helps to provide better quality at a lesser cost. Performance 
measurement is aligned with the NQS’s four priorities with two 
areas each: https://www.cms.gov/files/document/quality-motion-
cms-national-quality-strategy.pdf accessed 4/10/2025
 1. Promote Aligned and Improved Health Outcomes (Alignment: 
Align and Coordinate Across Programs and Care Settings; Out-
comes: Improve Quality and Health Outcomes Across the Care 
Journey),
 2. Advance Equity and Engagement for All Individuals (Equity: 
Advance Health Equity and Whole-Person Care; Engagement: 
Engage Individuals and Communities to Become Partners in 
Their Care),
 3. Ensure Safe and Resilient Health Care Systems (Safety: Achieve 
Zero Preventable Harm; Resiliency:  Enable a Responsive and 
Resilient Health Care System to Improve Quality) and
 4. Accelerate Interoperability and Scientific Innovation (Interopera-
bility: Accelerate and Support the T ransition to a Digital and 
Data-Driven Health Care System; Scientific Advancement: T rans-
form Health Care using Science, Analytics, and T echnology).
Over time, there has been a shift from performance improve -
ment being considered valuable for hospitals only, to being con -
sidered valuable for providers through relatable evidence-based 
data for clinicians. This is particularly useful in creating a culture 
of quality by aligning multidisciplinary teams, comprised of clini-
cians and nonclinicians, through joint review of evidence-based 
performance metrics and utilization of that data to work as a team 
on improving clinical care and processes.
A core guiding strategy underlying performance improvement 
is reduction in clinical process variation. Clinical variation in 
 patient care processes exists at every level of the US health care 
system. Electronic health records, computerized physician order 
entry, and the adverse drug effects alert system are advances 
achieved through information technology over the last decade to 
standardize care, prevent errors, and transform the quality of 
health care services delivered. Documentation of patient care, 
analysis of care patterns, and institutional- and provider-level feed-
back of these data have contributed to performance improvement 
and transparency regarding the quality of care provided, which 
consumers now seek, in order to purchase services based on their 
quality, meaning, and value-based purchasing.27 Within this per -
formance improvement paradigm, performance metrics can be 
considered as clinical process intelligence to provide decision 
 support, particularly in the management of chronic diseases. 
 Enterprise resource planning is moving to integrate diverse data -
bases such that clinical information will be available across points 
of care to individuals and communities and to local health care 
networks, ultimately leading to the evolution of a national  network.
Performance Improvement  
Methodology Overview
Health care performance improvement methodology has been 
adopted from other industries. Some examples of performance 
improvement frameworks include the Six Sigma cycle (define, 
measure, analyze, improve, control, 17 and plan-do-check-act 
[PDCA]). These performance improvement frameworks place a 
strong emphasis on identifying and using measures that accurately 
reflect processes and outcomes of the quality issue.
Performance improvement is a requirement by the Accredita -
tion Council for Graduate Medical Education of all residency 
programs, including PM&R, under the systems-based practice 
domain and has been elaborated upon in the latest Clinical Learn-
ing Environment Review (v2.0). 20 For PM&R residency pro -
grams, only a few quality improvement curricula to facilitate such 
learning are currently discussed in literature26 and show potential 
for more widespread adoption to improve systems citizenship skill-
set facilitation. After residency, the American Board of Physical 
Medicine and Rehabilitation (ABPMR) requires one performance 
improvement project to be completed every 5 years for continuing 
certification. The ABPMR project wizard offers some options for 
PM&R physicians to complete, or physicians may complete a 
project of their own design using guideline documents. The per -
formance improvement wizard PDCA methodology sections and 
set of questions are listed in Box 8.2. There are several ways to 
categorize quality improvement projects for ease of selection and 
performance using criteria such as quintuple aims (patient safety/
root cause analysis, patient experience, provider well-being, eq -
uity), care settings (inpatient, outpatient), and evaluation and 
management (assessment, interventions, pediatrics, medications). 
Examples of quality improvement projects under these categories 
are listed in Box 8.3. Quality improvement projects are described 
in greater detail in a later section of this chapter.
Policy Implications
Simply using these frameworks to fix problems or create solutions is 
inadequate.27 A change in thinking and organizational culture to 
seek and share organized information for patient safety is imperative 
as we move toward tying payment with quality. As more and more 
consumers expect evidence for the value of the care they receive, 
purchasers and payers are using performance measures to ensure 
quality while simultaneously controlling expenditures on health care 
with value-based performance. Since 2008, payers such as WellPoint 
and Aetna have followed in Medicare’s footsteps to differential reim-
bursement based on measurement and comparison. Performance 
metrics support pay-for-performance initiatives and accreditation in 
health plans. The American Academy of Physical Medicine and 
Rehabilitation (AAPM&R) in collaboration with PCPI recently 
developed measures that are relevant to PM&R. These measures are 
now available for use in the AAPM&R Registry.3,7

incentive program for health care quality administered by CMS. 
Through incentive payments, the QPP rewards attainment of bet-
ter health care outcomes and higher value by combining two prior 
quality-reporting programs into one system. There are two paths 
that providers can choose from to report quality data to CMS:  
(1) The Merit-Based Incentive Payment System (MIPS)

lines four prior independent programs: (a) EHR Incentive

ties. Using these components, a composite performance score is 
determined based on which eligible clinicians may receive a pay -
ment bonus, a payment penalty, or no payment adjustment de-
pending on the reporting year. MIPS is intended to ease clinician 
burden, compared to the prior quality programs, and allows 
 clinicians to choose the activities and measures that are most 
meaningful to their practice to demonstrate quality. Currently, 
most eligible clinicians are likely to initially participate in the 
QPP through MIPS to earn their payment incentive. (2) The 
second path to report quality data to CMS is the Alternate Pay -
ment Model (APM). The APM payment approach rewards high-
quality and cost-efficient care. APM can apply to a specific clinical 
condition, a care episode, or a population. Advanced APMs are a 
subset of APMs and let practices earn more for taking on some 
risk related to their patients’ outcomes. You may earn additional 
incentive payment by going further in improving patient care and 
taking on risk through an Advanced APM. Clinicians who choose 
Advanced APMs participate in innovative payment models.

Safety and Accreditation
Health plan accreditation standards are used to ensure that they 
are performing on par with industry standards in quality by mea-
suring their performance. Consumers use these data to compare 
plans and shop, as do other purchasers, such as employers and 
state and federal regulators, who utilize these performance metrics 
so they can pay for performance. The NCQA, Utilization Review 
Accreditation Commission, and The Joint Commission are such 
agencies. These agencies rank insurance plans by using tools such 
as the Medicare Health Outcomes Survey, in which all managed 
care organizations holding Medicare Advantage contracts are re -
quired to enroll in HEDIS. AHRQ’s Consumer Assessment of 
Health Providers and Systems is another such tool.
The Leapfrog Group advocates for quality and safety in hospitals 
with transparency in pricing and reports on several performance 
measures. They compare hospitals in terms of preventing medication 
errors, appropriate intensive care unit staffing, steps to avoid harm, 
managing serious errors, safety-focused scheduling, and hospital-
acquired conditions and provide a Hospital Safety Grade available to 
consumers on their website.37 Performance metrics data from the 
CMS are available through its Hospital Compare website.40
The accreditation process for inpatient medical rehabilitation 
facilities through the Commission on the Accreditation of Reha -
bilitation Facilities (CARF) is internationally recognized as the 
industry standard for enhancing care in rehabilitation facilities. 39 
CARF standards are developed through a rigorous process, which 
includes input from leaders in rehabilitation, stakeholders, and 
patients served and their families. CARF has defined its mission to 
“promote quality, value and optimal outcomes of services through 
their peer review consultative accreditation process and continuous 
improvement services with the goal of enhancing the lives of per-
sons served.” CARF emphasizes the importance of participation of 
the persons served in their process and utilizes the Universal Stake-
holder Participation and Experience Questionnaire to gather input 
from stakeholders to identify areas that need improvement and 
drive change in addition to their standards. CARF uses ASPIRE to 
Excellence as the framework for its standards to measure perfor -
mance and assess management and quality.15
Public reporting by CMS on nursing homes and measuring the 
performance of home- and community-based services with finan-
cial incentives linked have been steps toward improving health care 
quality in the long-term and postacute care arenas. Physician per-
formance metrics are tied into financial incentives. The public re-
porting of performance metrics is a driver of health care quality 
because it encourages transparency and accountability.
Evidence-Based Medicine
EBM practice is considered an integral part of providing quality 
and cost-effective care.21 EBM has been defined as “the integration 
of best research evidence with clinical expertise and patient val -
ues.”51 Utilizing EBM, physicians can address clinical questions by 
assessing the quality of evidence in medical information available 
in both basic science and clinical research. 63 Sackett et al. 52 also 
explained EBM as the integration of individuals’ clinical expertise 
with the best available external evidence from systematic research 
and recommended using EBM as “the conscientious, explicit, and 
judicious use of current best evidence in making decisions about 
the care of individual patients.”
It is important to acknowledge the limitations of making 
clinical decisions solely on one’s knowledge of pathophysiology of 
disease processes, clinical intuition, and individual clinical experi-
ence. With the EBM approach, practitioners can use individual 
clinical knowledge and acumen, careful history taking, and 
physical examination skills before evaluating existing evidence 
and combining it with the clinical information obtained in a 
systematic and reproducible manner. 23 Clinical decisions made 
using the EBM approach are more reproducible and freer of bias 
than the traditional approach. Another advantage to this ap -
proach is that it allows a clinician to adapt to and accept new 
scientific evidence more easily. The clinician is enabled to apply 
the new evidence at the appropriate point in the medical decision-
making algorithm to make corresponding changes to one’s clinical 
practice. Equipped with the best new evidence, the clinician can 
tailor a treatment plan that aligns also with the patient’s individ-
ual goals and values using a shared decision-making process with 
the patient.
Assessing, Evaluating, and Applying Evidence
Sackett et al. 51 recommend application of EBM using a simple 
and straightforward approach to answer any clinical question by 
(1) defining a clinical question, (2) finding the evidence that will 
help to answer the question, (3) assessing whether this evidence 
is valid and important, and (4) applying the evidence to the 
patient.
For PM&R clinicians trying to apply available scientific evi -
dence to their practice, this means that they would first have to be 
aware of the existing evidence base in the area of interest. In topic 
areas where there are few published research articles, this task can 
be accomplished by performing a basic literature search and as -
sessing the evidence base of the intervention in the articles re -
viewed. However, as is often the case, several published articles 
may exist in the topic area of interest, frequently with conflicting 
results. In such an instance, a review article published in this area 
often reveals a summary of evidence collated from published re -
search in the area of interest.
In general, the level of evidence provided by a published article 
can be determined from the level-of-evidence hierarchy (Fig. 8.5). 
However, one can also appraise the level of evidence of a research 
article using evidence tables. The Oxford Center for Evidence-
Based Medicine (OCEBM) has an easy-to-use table that can help 
to determine the level of evidence of a published article. 45 The 
OCEBM is widely accepted and well regarded in the scientific 
community. The table adopted by the AAPM&R has five levels 
for each of four types of studies: therapeutic, prognostic, diagnos-
tic, and economic/decision modeling. 64 Using either table, 
 reviewers can evaluate the level of evidence of the articles they 
review to research a clinical question. Once aware of the evidence, 
the clinician would then have to “conscientiously, explicitly, and 
judiciously” apply such evidence in making clinical decisions 
about the care of individual patients.

For a PM&R clinician reading an original research article or a 
review article and wondering how it might alter the management 
of a specific patient, another potentially useful EBM concept is 
the evidence-based triad.
25 This concept provides a guide for de-
ciding on the applicability of evidence to individual patients by 
seeking common ground in the triad of (1) clinical impression 
based on individual clinical expertise, (2) best available external 
evidence, and (3) the patient’s unique values and expectations 
(Fig. 8.6).
When it comes to using EBM to answer a clinical diagnostic 
decision-making question, the following stepwise approach has 
been recommended
65: (1) Define a clinical question and its four 
components: patient, intervention, comparison, and outcome. (2) 
Find the available evidence that will help answer the question. (3) 
Assess whether this evidence is valid and important. (4) Apply the 
evidence to the patient. This last step includes assessing whether 
the test can be used, determining if it will help the patient, finding 
whether the study patients are similar to the patient in question, 
determining a pretest probability, and deciding if the test will 
change the management of the patient (Fig. 8.7).
It is recognized that the key steps of EBM practice require 
some training. EBM has been adopted as part of PM&R resi -
dency training over the past 20 years. Annaswamy et al.

Clinical Practice Guidelines
Busy clinicians may find it challenging to appraise literature for 
every clinical decision. T ools such as review articles and CPGs can 
be helpful to clinicians who wish to implement evidence-based 
practice.
CPGs are systematically developed written statements made 
after a systematic evaluation of available evidence and intended to 
assist practitioners and patients in making appropriate decisions 
about health care in specific conditions or circumstances. Broadly 
speaking, CPGs aim to (1) describe most appropriate care based 
on the best available evidence, (2) reduce inappropriate variation 
in practice for a specified condition, (3) provide a more rational 
basis for interventions and referrals, (4) promote efficient use of 
health care resources, (5) act as a point of focus for quality control 
and audit, (6) highlight shortcomings of existing literature and 
suggest appropriate future research, and (7) provide an impetus 
for continuing education.
The schematic shown in Fig. 8.8 provides a model for how 
evidence-based guidelines are created. The IOM, in response to a 
request from the US Congress, formulated a report on the best 
methods to be used in developing CPGs that are rigorous and 
trustworthy. The IOM’s eight standards for developing CPGs are 
considered the gold standard that every CPG aims to reach. CPGs 
critical to the field of PM&R that developed, advocated for, and 
were endorsed by the AAPM&R are available for review as well.
6 
Cochrane Rehabilitation is an online resource available for reha -
bilitation professionals, wherein Cochrane reviews and recom -
mendations for clinical use on topics pertinent to rehabilitation 
are developed and available for use.
19 Cochrane Rehabilitation 
proposes to help identify commonly used rehabilitation treat -
ments that are well supported based on practice-based evidence; 
they do not require formal evidentiary support and offer a list of 
rehabilitation interventions that are evidence based despite lack of 
support from randomized controlled trials.
44
James M. Anderson Center for Health Systems Excellence has 
a division dedicated to support EBM decision making and pro -
vides topic-specific evidence-based care recommendations as CPGs 
relevant to pediatric care.
32 CPGs relevant to management of pa-
tients with spinal cord injury, based on evidence-based research 
conducted by the North American Consortium for Spinal Cord 
Medicine, are published by the Paralyzed Veterans of America.
46
The Veterans Health Administration in collaboration with the 
Department of Defense has published guidelines that include 
management of upper and lower limb amputations and postacute 
mild traumatic brain injury. AAPM&R‘s Ethical Health Check 
Questionnaire committee reviews PM&R-relevant guidelines on 
a cyclic basis. At the time of this publication, the AAPM&R  -
endorsed guidelines include management of anterior cruciate liga-
ment injuries, hip fractures, glenohumeral joint arthritis, among 
others.

Continuing Board Certification and  
Quality Improvement
The ABPMR is one of the 24-member boards of the American 
Board of Medical Specialties (ABMS). Since 1993, all certificates 
issued by the ABPMR were to be recertified every 10 years; in 
2022, the process changed to a 5-year continuing certification 
cycle. The recertification process had been elevated to a more in -
volved process of maintenance of certification before 2020 and 
now continuing certification. The change has taken place in an 
effort to assure the public that board-certified specialists have re -
mained current with evolving knowledge, that their practices 
meet acceptable quality and safety standards, and that they are 
recognized and respected as specialists by their patients and peers. 
This ongoing process is designed to verify a diplomate’s creden -
tials, licensure, professional standing, and practice performance. 
Maintenance of and continuing certifications are intended to be 
quality drivers, but their relevance and value to society remain 
under scrutiny and are being challenged
16 even today.
The ABPMR continuing certification program has one pri -
mary certification and six specialty certifications (directly offers 
brain injury medicine, pediatric rehabilitation medicine, and 
spinal cord injury medicine; cosponsors neuromuscular medicine, 
pain medicine, and sports medicine). For each of the three

provides a mechanism for measuring, on a continual basis, the 
diplomate’s competencies in the PM&R specialty. T o maintain 
time-limited certification, diplomates are required to complete 
four components that measure the six competencies (Box 8.4).
The ABPMR states that the standards of certification are dis -
tinct from those of licensure. Possession of a board certificate does 
not indicate total qualification for practice privileges, nor does it 
imply exclusion of other physicians who are not so certified.
Diplomates’ continuing certification participation status, ei-
ther “meeting requirements” or “not meeting requirements” will 
be available to the public when using the searchable “Verify a 
Physician’s Certification” tool on the ABPMR website.
Even though the maintenance of and continuing certification 
programs sound rational and generally seem well designed to ad -
vance medical knowledge and promote quality improvement 
among health care providers, the effectiveness, meaningfulness, 
and cost effectiveness of the recertification programs have not 
been proven scientifically.
62 The eventual goal is that with greater 
transparency and accountability, a board-certified diplomate of 
any specialty will be held to equal standards. Through these pro -
grams, board-certified physicians are expected to advance the 
quality and standard of health care nationwide. The ABMS be -
lieves higher standards mean better care and considers the certifi-
cation programs an essential element of this process. The ABPMR

Moving From the Maintenance of Certification 
to the Continuous Certification Program
According to the ABMS, diplomates have raised major concerns 
about maintenance of certification programs. Recognizing that a 
comprehensive revision to this certification process was required, 
the ABMS has reviewed both the framework and purpose of con-
tinuing certification of physicians and launched a process, entitled 
Continuing Board Certification: Vision for the Future (The Vision 
Initiative).8 After extensive research done by The Vision Initiative 
Commission of ABMS, the ABMS has recommended continu -
ance of the certification program with a robust, evidence-based, 
efficient, and fair approach. The name maintenance of certifica -
tion has been replaced by the newer term, continuing certification 
program.
T o address similar concerns raised by its diplomates, the 
 ABPMR implemented in January 2020 the Longitudinal 
Assessment for PM&R (LA-PM&R), representing a novel 
approach to ABPMR certification that is more focused on 
learning and continuous improvement. According to the ABPMR, 
the LA-PM&R will:
 1. Replace the secure maintenance of certification part III 
 examination
 2. Count for self-assessment continuing medical education 
( SA-CME) requirements (earn up to 12 SA-CME credits per 
year of LA-PM&R participation)
 3. Be part of your 5-year continuing certification program re -
quirements, which also include professionalism, lifelong learn-
ing (CME), and quality improvement
 4. Ask 15 to 20 questions per quarter
 5. Provide customized content based on five domains
 6. Provide immediate feedback and learning resources
Participants can customize the LA-PM&R content by adjust -
ing the relative percentages of the following categories:
• Central nervous system rehabilitation: brain injury, spinal cord 
injury, and stroke
• Musculoskeletal and pain medicine: arthritis, acute/chronic 
trauma, fractures, spinal disorders, and sports medicine
• Neuromuscular medicine/electrodiagnosis: electromyogra -
phy, neuropathies, nerve conduction studies, neuromuscular 
 transmission
• Medical rehabilitation: amputation, cancer, cardiopulmonary, 
infectious disease, genitourinary/gastrointestinal, and transplant
• Pediatric rehabilitation medicine: related topics
LA-PM&R is open to all diplomates as part of the continuing 
certification program in PM&R as of January 2020. 11,12
Quality Improvement Projects and  
Project Options
As one of six competencies of ABPMR, practice-based learning 
and improvement is addressed by the continuing certification 
program. Its diplomates must demonstrate the ability to investi -
gate and evaluate the care of patients, appraise and assimilate sci -
entific evidence, and continuously improve patient care based on 
constant self-evaluation and lifelong learning. Diplomates with 
time-limited certificates issued between 2012 and 2019 must 
complete two ABPMR-approved practice performance projects 
(one project in years 1–5 and one project in years 6–10) during 
the 10-year maintenance of certification cycle. Diplomates with 
certificates issued in 2020 and beyond must complete one 
 ABPMR-approved quality improvement project during each 
5-year continuing certification cycle (Table 8.2).12
The ABPMR suggested three common options before year 
2020 for fulfilling the practice improvement project requirement, 
and since then three more options have been added. 10 The term 
practice improvement project  has been replaced with quality im -
provement project. There are several quality improvement project 
options available to PM&R physicians through various profes -
sional organizations (see later).
ABPMR Self-Directed Quality Improvement Project
The ABPMR has developed a quality improvement project wizard 
that guides diplomates to pertinent quality improvement project 
options based on their self-identified clinical areas of practice. 9

The wizard offers more than 20 projects for users to choose that 
encompass quality improvements not only in clinical areas but 
also in such things as administration, education, and research. 
The self-directed quality improvement project allows individual 
diplomates the opportunity to demonstrate improvement within 
their own practice using a continuous quality improvement pro -
cess and PDSA methodology. This option is free of charge.
American Academy of Physical Medicine and 
Rehabilitation Preformatted Quality Improvement Project
AAPM&R offers four practice improvement projects on deep vein 
thrombosis, stroke rehabilitation, osteoporosis, and low back 
pain. After deciding on a project, the diplomate will receive a 
toolkit to guide the process. The diplomate starts the process by 
answering a series of set questions: self-evaluation of an issue, 
practice-based assessment of the issue, and self-selection of three 
areas for improvement from a predetermined list based on an -
swers to the self-evaluation and practice-based assessment ques -
tions. All diplomates create an improvement program specific to 
their practice with a variety of tools and resources available.
The non-AAPM&R member diplomate must pay a fee for this 
service and earn 20 CME credit hours. Minimum 30-day period 
of data collection and analysis is recommended.
American Association of Neuromuscular and 
Electrodiagnostic Medicine Performance in Practice
The American Association of Neuromuscular and Electrodiagnos-
tic Medicine (AANEM) is an organization designed to enhance 
quality patient care by focusing on providing quality electrodiag -
nostic (EDX) services. It has three performance-in-practice proj -
ects available: ulnar neuropathy, carpal tunnel syndrome, and 
EDX report writing. Each project was formed from an expert 
consensus document created by AANEM members. According to 
AANEM, the purpose of the quality improvement projects is to 
enable a physician to apply best practices in the electrodiagnosis 
of ulnar neuropathy, carpal tunnel syndrome, or EDX report writ-
ing. Each module will help physicians evaluate the quality of their 
EDX practice on the specific topic and identify areas of potential 
improvement and implement the action for changes.
A non-AANEM diplomate pays a fee for this service and earns 
20 CME credit hours. A minimal 30-day period of data collection 
and analysis is recommended.
American Board of Medical Specialties Portfolio Program
The ABMS Portfolio Program works with many types of health 
care organizations to recognize the work physicians are already 
doing to improve their practices and the care of their patients. The 
sponsors (health care organizations) support physician involve -
ment in local improvement initiatives by awarding credit for 
continuing certification from a participating ABMS member 
board, including ABPMR. Only a diplomate whose affiliated or -
ganization is on the ABMS sponsor list can use this program for 
the quality improvement credit.
National Committee on Quality Assurance Patient-
Centered Specialty Practice Recognition
The NCQA Patient-Centered Specialty Practice Recognition pro-
gram builds on the success of the PCMH recognition program  
by recognizing specialty practices that excel in delivering high-
quality, patient-centered care. It focuses on proactive coordination 
and sharing of information. Everyone in the practice works as a 
team to coordinate care with primary care, other referring 
 clinicians, community resources, and secondary services. This 
project is done at an organizational level with purchased recogni -
tion service from NCQA.
The American Institute of Ultrasound in 
Medicine Practice Accreditation in 
Musculoskeletal Ultrasound-Guided 
Interventional Procedures
The American Institute of Ultrasound in Medicine (AIUM) ultra-
sound practice accreditation allows practices to demonstrate that 
they meet or exceed nationally recognized standards in the perfor-
mance and interpretation of diagnostic ultrasound examinations. 
A diplomate needs to submit both an application and case study 
documents to be evaluated for accreditation. The accreditation 
process is a paid service. ABPMR accepts the AIUM practice ac -
creditation in musculoskeletal ultrasound-guided interventional 
procedure as a quality improvement project credit.
Summary
With a team-based, patient-centric, health care quality mission of 
safe, efficient, cost-effective, and equitable health care for all, 
 rehabilitation professionals, including physiatrists, must work 
 together to achieve best care quality and outcomes for patients. 
Practicing safe EBM that is focused on value and outcomes can 
help us get there. Fair, transparent, valid, and objective measure -
ments of quality and safety should be embraced. It is together that 
we need to continue our journey to an accessible, affordable, 
high-quality health care system that is valuable and sustainable 
over the long term for all.
